data.title: BNDI: Bonds And Equity Spreads ETF, Above-Average 5.7% Yield, Great Track-Record
data.description:
I recently wrote an article on the Neos Enhanced Income Cash Alternative ETF (CSHI), which focuses on t-bills, and with smaller equity option spreads meant to boost income. The NEOS Enhanced Income Aggregate Bond ETF (BNDI) is similar to CSHI, with similar spreads, but focusing on bonds.

BDNI's strategy boosts the fund's dividend yield to 5.7%, around 1.8% higher than its benchmark, with a smaller increase in past and expected returns. BNDI's strategy should result in small, single-digit losses during significant, sharp equity drawdowns. In my opinion, BNDI's increased income more than outweighs its higher risk, making the fund a buy.

Quick Strategy Overview

Quick overview of BNDI's strategy before looking into some of the fund's characteristics, advantages and disadvantages.

BNDI focuses on bonds, through equal investments in the Vanguard Total Bond Market Index Fund ETF Shares (BND) and the iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG). Both these ETFs track the same broad-based bond index, and account for around 99.5% of the fund's assets. BNDI's management fees are set up so that total operating expenses, including any expenses paid to BND and AGG, add up to 0.58%. Although fees are not excessive, they are definitely above-average, and a drag on the fund's income and performance.

BNDI does some equity option spreads, with these accounting for only 0.5% of its assets, but a greater proportion of its income, returns, and (potential) risks and drawdowns. Let's have a closer look at these spreads, take special note of the highlighted positions:

In the example above, BNDI bought and sold put options on the S&P 500.

Selling the put option generates some money in premiums for the fund, boosting its dividend yield. Selling the option also means losses if the S&P 500 were to decline from its 6,600 strike price by its 05/07/2026 expiration date, next week. The S&P 500 trades at a 7,169 price right now, so the index would have to decline by around 8% before the fund starts to experience any losses from these options. Breakevens are a bit lower, on account of the premiums. It is rare for the S&P 500 to experience losses of this magnitude in the space of a week or two, so it should be rare for BNDI to experience any losses from these options.

To further limit risks, the fund bought another put option, this one with a 6,325 strike price, same expiration date. Said option means profits for the fund if the S&P 500 were to decline below 6,325, capping losses at said level. On the flipside, buying said option is costly, reducing the fund's income.

In general, investors should expect BNDI to experience small, single-digit losses from significant, sharp S&P 500 drawdowns, with an increase in dividends in the low single-digits to compensate for the added risk. Specifics vary depending on the specifics of the trades done, but that is the general gist of this strategy.

Although BNDI is mostly a simple bond ETF, it is these options which set the fund apart from its benchmark, in both positive and negative ways. Let's have a look at these.

Benchmark Comparison

Positives and Advantages

BNDI's most important advantage versus its benchmark is its 5.7% dividend yield, quite a bit higher than BND and AGG's 3.9% figure. Spreads here are due to the fund's options and are net of fees. Spreads fluctuate according to option premiums and amounts, and due to standard ETF dividend volatility. Spreads varied between 1.7% and 2.0% last year but were quite a bit tighter before. Would include a standard YCharts dividend yield graph here, but the data for BNDI seems inconsistent with figures from the fund itself and Seeking Alpha.

BNDI's above-average dividend yield should ensure long-term outperformance, as has been the case since inception. Outperformance amounts to 1.2% per year, on average, somewhat lower than expected, even after accounting for prior spreads.

In my opinion and considering BNDI's performance track-record and that of CSHI, outperformance should be closer to 1.2% from here on out than to the 1.8% difference in dividend yields. Why the fund seems to be over-distributing a bit of income or premiums is unclear to me, but not uncommon for option ETFs. Could be simply volatility too, as the figures have materially improved since I originally published the article on the CEF/ETF Income Laboratory.

In any case, BNDI's above-average dividend yield is an important benefit for shareholders, and advantage relative to peers.

Negatives and Disadvantages

BNDI's most important disadvantage versus its benchmark is its equity exposure. As mentioned previously, BNDI's options should result in small, single-digit losses during significant, sharp equity drawdowns. Losses could be permanent, assuming the S&P 500 doesn't recover quickly enough (reasonable enough assumption).

In practice, it is extremely rare for the S&P 500 to suffer drawdowns sharp enough to result in sizable, or permanent, losses for BNDI. As an example, the equity bear market in early 2025 was quite sharp, with double-digit drawdowns during the first week or two of April, preceded by several weeks of more muted losses. The recovery was sharp too but took around two months. BDNI experienced around 2.0% in greater losses than BND during early April, was almost recovered by late April, fully recovered the next month. BNDI's performance looks even better on the graph.

As should be clear from the above, BNDI does have some equity risk, but not a whole lot. In my opinion and considering both recent equity bear markets and BNDI's options strategy, the fund should see negligible medium-term losses during the vast majority of downturns and recessions, although there should be a bit of short-term volatility.

As mentioned previously, I believe that BNDI's excess income more than outweighs its added risks. It is simply a respectable amount of income for a small amount of capped, rare risk. Looking at the fund's total returns since inception, the risk is barely noticable.

Risk considerations are obviously strongly dependent on the investor, and I'm sure some will consider the small added risk here to be undesirable, some a deal-breaker.

Finally, BNDI's 0.58% expense ratio is a straightforward disadvantage relative to its benchmark and peers. Expenses here are far from excessive but are material enough to be a drag on its performance and income. In my opinion, these expenses are extremely unlike to make or break BNDI as an investment: its equity exposure is. Still, some investors might prefer focusing on cheaper funds.

Other Considerations

BNDI and CSHI are similar ETFs from the same issuer. Due to their similarities, thought a quick comparison was in order.

CSHI focuses on t-bills compared to bonds for BNDI. Both have similar option strategies.

BNDI's bond focus means a higher dividend yield and returns, greater volatility. BNDI's bond focus might make the added risk more palatable to investors. Those small excess losses during early 2025 might not mean much for a bond ETF, or for bond investors, but they very well might mean a lot to investors in cash, or CSHI. A bit of an odd consideration but thought to mention it as I had discussed CSHI so recently.

BNDI's equity risk/excess income tradeoff seems quite good compared to high-yield corporate bonds. BNDI's income is somewhere between that of its benchmark and these riskier bonds:

It is difficult to quantify the differences in risks here, but I think BNDI's risk is much lower. Losses are capped, for starters, while that is technically not the case for high-yield bonds (realistically the government would intervene at some point, but much higher than single-digit losses). BNDI should not suffer any losses from slower, shallower equity bear markets, while USHY's price would simply slowly decline (assuming said bear market coincides with either bearish sentiment or declining economic conditions, which seems likely).

BNDI's performance was somewhere between that of BND and high-yield corporate bond benchmarks during early 2025. Results are fine, with BNDI being between these two investments on both income and drawdowns, but I expected better. I continue to expect better though, 2025 seems unusually sharp, and barely qualifies as a downturn.

Looking Forward

Looking forward, I see no reason to expect a deterioration in BNDI's strategy or performance.

BNDI's bond exposure comes through an investment in a simple, high-quality, bond index ETF. Federal Reserve rates remain elevated, so bonds continue to offer good income to investors. Interest rate risk is the most important risk here, but guidance is for zero cuts next year, one cut next year, a scenario which doesn't seem likely to significantly impact BNDI. Credit quality is high enough for credit risk to not be a significant consideration. Quality for BND is as follows:

Simply put, BNDI's portfolio seems fine, and I see no reason to expect a deterioration here moving forward.

BNDI's equity option spreads are dependent on many factors, including their specifics (strike prices, expiration dates, etc.), which are at management's discretion, as well as timing and option prices, the latter of these in turn dependent on (implied) bond volatility. Not much to say here besides the fact that I don't foresee a deterioration in these moving forward.

Although the section here might seem a bit vague, due remember that BNDI sports an above-average 5.7% dividend yield, and that it should consistently trade at a spread to its benchmark. That should result in outperformance barring some adverse scenario for the fund, say another pandemic or financial crisis, but I'm simply not expecting any such scenario, which means I expect the fund to continue to outperform moving forward.

Conclusion

BNDI focuses on bonds, with smaller equity option spread trades meant to boost income, while exposing the fund to a small amount of equity risk. BNDI's strategy has led to an above-average 5.7% dividend yield for the fund, and consistent outperformance relative to peers, without a significant impact on risk or drawdowns. I'm quite bullish on the fund and its strategy, and rate it a buy. More risk-averse investors might disagree.

data.title: Riverwater Micro Opportunities Strategy Q1 2026 Portfolio Activity
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Riverwater Micro Opportunities Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary.

Top Contributors & Detractors

Top Contributors

NPK International (NPKI) was the strategy's leading contributor during the quarter. NPKI outperformed the market in the first quarter of 2026, driven by increasing investor recognition of its role as a "picks and shovels" beneficiary of the ongoing energy and infrastructure buildout, including power demand tied to AI-driven data center expansion. The company's composite matting solutions are critical for supporting grid expansion, pipeline development, and broader infrastructure projects, positioning it well in a capital spending environment that remains resilient despite macro uncertainty. Investors have also responded positively to the company's long runway for growth, as NPKI continues to expand its rental fleet, providing a clear path to incremental revenue and margin expansion. Importantly, the composite mat industry is growing faster than traditional wood-based alternatives, driven by superior durability, environmental benefits, and lower total cost of ownership. This combination of structural growth, capacity expansion, and exposure to durable infrastructure themes has supported both earnings visibility and multiple expansion during the quarter.

Douglas Dynamics (PLOW) was another standout and a company we highlighted in the deep dive section of the fourth quarter of 2025 letter. PLOW is a leading North American manufacturer of commercial snow and ice control equipment and work truck solutions, serving municipal, commercial, and contractor end markets. The bottom line is that this winter's snow season was considerably more robust than last year's and exceeded the strength of most typical winters. This favorable weather significantly improved the financial results and fundamental outlook for Douglas Dynamics.

Top Detractors

Red Violet (RDVT), a provider of data analytics and identity intelligence solutions used across government, law enforcement, and enterprise markets, underperformed in the first quarter of 2026 primarily due to broad-based weakness across the software sector. Much of the sell-off was driven by investor concerns around AI disruption, which has led to indiscriminate multiple compression across data and analytics companies. Despite this pressure, we believe RDVT's competitive positioning remains intact, supported by its proprietary data assets and highly specialized identity intelligence capabilities. These datasets, which have been built over time and are difficult to replicate, create a meaningful barrier to entry and underpin the company's value proposition. In our view, the recent underperformance reflects sentiment rather than fundamentals, and the company remains well positioned to benefit from continued demand for high-quality data and analytics solutions.

Coastal Financial (CCB) was our second largest detractor. CCB experienced a more volatile first quarter, with stock performance shaped by evolving investor perceptions around risks tied to its fintech partner ecosystem and broader private credit dynamics. While underlying fundamentals remained relatively stable—supported by its Banking-as-a-Service model and continued growth in partner-driven deposits and fee income—investors grew more focused on the durability of its fintech relationships amid increasing AI-driven disruption and tightened credit conditions. We reduced our position during the quarter but continue to hold CCB at a lower weighting, as we believe the core business model remains intact.

Strategy Additions & Sales

New Positions

We initiated positions in several companies during the quarter that align with our focus on quality businesses trading at attractive valuations.

Ranger Energy Services (RNGR) is a leading provider of high-spec well service rigs and ancillary services to U.S. onshore oil and gas operators, with a focus on completion, production, and maintenance activity across key basins such as the Permian. The investment case is centered on its leverage to sustained and potentially increasing North American oil and gas production, particularly in an environment where global supply uncertainty—driven in part by geopolitical tensions—supports higher commodity prices and incentivizes domestic output. Unlike exploration and production companies, RNGR offers a more stable, service-oriented exposure to the energy cycle, benefiting from ongoing well maintenance and workover activity that is less volatile than drilling. As producers seek to maximize existing asset productivity and extend well life, demand for high-quality service rigs remains robust, positioning RNGR to drive utilization and pricing improvements. Combined with disciplined capital allocation and a focus on operational efficiency, the company is well positioned to generate strong free cash flow and deliver attractive returns through the cycle.

We initiated a position in Talkspace (TALK), a digital mental health platform with improving unit economics. TALK was taken out shortly after we made the purchase, providing an immediate return. The acquisition was bittersweet: while the takeout delivered strong performance on the day, we believed the company's improving fundamentals offered more upside over a longer horizon.

We added LSB Industries (LXU) in the first quarter. We added to existing positions in Climb Global Solutions (CLMB), Allot Ltd. (ALLT), Natural Gas Services Group (NGS), and American Vanguard (AVD), taking advantage of price dislocations from the AI disruption thesis or to build positions that we believe will benefit from the new macro environment.

On the sell side, we trimmed IRadimed (IRMD), Douglas Dynamics (PLOW), Coastal Financial (CCB), Delcath Systems (DCTH), First Business Financial (FBIZ), and Avidia Bancorp (AVBC). The trims to IRMD and PLOW were primarily valuation-driven following strong performance. We trimmed our bank positions based on the possibility of less rate hikes and the worries about private credit hitting the entire Financials sector.

Disclosures: The information contained herein represents the opinion of Riverwater and should not be construed as personalized or individualized investment advice. Analysis and opinion expressed in this report are subject to change without notice. Reader should not assume that investments in the securities identified were or will be profitable. Timing differences of purchases and sales may have a modest impact on the actual contribution numbers presented. The holdings identified do not represent all the securities purchased, sold, or recommended. The calculation's methodology along with details on all holding's contribution to the overall account's performance during the measurement period are available upon request. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

The contributions presented are shown on a gross basis and do not reflect the deduction of management fees, or other fees. The deduction of management fees reduces investment returns and would also affect the investment characteristics presented. Each contributor's share of performance is shown for informational purposes only and is not predictive of future performance or investment outcomes.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Micron: Understanding If That Was The Mid-Cycle Correction
data.description:
Micron (MU) has become the front-and-center darling of the AI boom as earnings have not just become historic but entirely inflected to otherworldly levels. It was only a few years ago I was writing about the potential for Micron to become a primary beneficiary of the AI advancement. While the story is more nuanced than HBM (high bandwidth memory), a lot of bulls have run toward valuation metrics and other means to figure out where Micron is going and even when. Telling newcomers to stop looking at P/E ratios is where I left off, and I said then the stock had more to go before hitting my target based on sentiment, not valuation. Now, after hitting and exceeding it and then seeing a sharp correction in Q1, the question is whether Micron just completed what many of us have experienced in other cycles as a mid-cycle correction or if the push in these last couple of weeks was all there is to Micron's cycle rally.

To answer this requires understanding sentiment in Micron.

The Bull of Stocks

Micron is notorious for being a tricky and sometimes cunning stock to deal with, akin to a bull rider trying to predict a bull's temperament. The root cause with Micron is its ending diagonal style pattern on a year's level degree. The oscillations are large and sometimes very intense. In practice, Micron's oscillations are not in line with standard rallies and selloffs.

Sure, we can talk about EPS growth, consensus revisions, and margins all day long, but none of it matters to the stock price as long as they're heading higher. Fundamentals play a role, just not the role you've been told to believe. What matters is the sentiment behind its stock.

Think about it, all of these extremely bullish business fundamentals were known a month ago when the stock was at three-month lows. In fact, it got to the three-month lows on its historic report. It didn't make a difference then, and it doesn't make a difference now. The same fundamentals were at the three-month lows as they are at all-time highs this week. This is because sentiment moves in waves and cycles, and it was still working through a corrective cycle.

The question is whether this corrective cycle has completed or whether it was just the first leg of a larger one. As of last week, as you'll see shortly, the former is now the higher probability.

Let's Answer The Question

Some of you may be reading this and referring back to my prior article, saying, "Well, you only had a target of $360 back then, so what changed?"

There are two major points, and the first is that the market price action is always the source of truth. I base the analysis on what it provides, not what I hope it will provide. The second is the point I made earlier about Micron being unorthodox in its setups and patterns. Where the standard is for five waves in the direction of the trend and three waves against the trend, Micron has built a large setup of three waves in both directions at a lot of junctures. This has meant I have to take each completion of a wave structure with caution because the next move is going to provide the same wave structure, whether it continues higher or has topped. It becomes a matter of support levels and analyzing the lower degrees to understand when we're in a more risk-adjusted setup.

After the high in late January, I was expecting a correction, but the size and degree of it weren't clear at the time. In this larger C wave (part of that three-wave rally pattern), it could have been the third wave of five. The question I wanted to know is whether it entered the fourth wave of the C wave or just a pullback still within the third wave. If it entered the fourth wave, the targets were fairly low, reaching as low as the mid-$200s. But fourth waves don't always adhere to the standard setups, and since Micron already is a wild horse (or bull, as it were), it wouldn't be surprising to see the fourth wave stop short.

We did get the correction I expected, but it landed at a low of $311, and it included an expanded B wave correction (meaning the correction provided a higher high above the high before the correction began). This data is important because it foreshadows a delayed bullish reaction once the correction completes. This meant I should be on the lookout for the correction to resolve sooner rather than later and not be ongoing. I shared this with Tech Cache subscribers as it was unfolding in real-time.

And this may be exactly what we have seen after this rally into all-time highs.

So Is It Complete?

But don't be fooled; just because it pushed into all-time highs isn't highly relevant to the increased probabilities. The structure is what determines the confidence in the count, along with the structure and levels held for what comes next. The reason why my primary count (green) is now the 1-2 wave count for higher is due to the five waves up I've seen off the $311 low.

However, while that was the more difficult task, it isn't confirmed until I see a three-wave pullback. And this three-wave pullback should hold the 61.8% retrace, currently at $382. This is the lower end of the green box, and notice I said hold it, because it doesn't have to make it all the way there to complete wave 2. The upper end of the box is the 38.2% retrace, which is usually the minimum target for a second wave, currently at $433. As long as the three-wave pullback doesn't meaningfully exceed the 61.8% retrace yet is large enough (the 38.2%) to consider the stock in the next higher degree, we have upside targets to $700 for the next immediate target in wave three of three (100% extension).

That's the more probable bull case right now.

But if price action gives us something else (see my first major point from earlier), then the probabilities will shift back to the deeper fourth wave I had initially. This can either be in the form of the yellow count, which would be noticeable as it would be five waves down off the high for circle C, or the teal count, which means the third wave ended on the five up we've gotten over the last couple of weeks, and the larger correction has just started. The teal count is less discernible as it would start as three waves down, exactly like green, except it won't hold the green support box (it would also show three waves off the support box if it did, not the five up needed for green).

As Micron heads into its earnings report in a little less than two months, the second wave would be a prime setup for a pullback. The report could act as a third wave catalyst, higher. This would be different from March's report because it was the catalyst to drive the stock to three-month lows. The reason why this report, which may be a more modest beat and raise, could launch the stock higher is because of where sentiment is positioned ahead of it. The key to understanding is the earnings report doesn't determine direction; sentiment is already in motion before it and can merely be a trigger or catalyst to send it on the next wave.

We'll know if this was the infamous mid-cycle correction we've seen in the past in the coming weeks, as this second wave is confirmed or invalidated.

Overall, the setup is fairly clear at this point. The pullback, as long as it follows the structure needed, can be a huge buy-the-dip moment. You'll notice even with the call I just laid out, I have this rated as a hold. That's because I need to see the confirmation of the pullback first before I add to my long position, and I may not have another opportunity to publish another public article to upgrade it to buy once it happens. If it does pull back as I've outlined here, consider it an unreflected buy.

data.title: LSB Industries: A Compelling Small-Cap Opportunity
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Riverwater Micro Opportunities Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary.

LSB Industries (LXU) is a U.S.-based pure-play nitrogen chemical producer operating three manufacturing facilities in Alabama, Arkansas, and Oklahoma, along with a nitric acid facility in Baytown, Texas operated on behalf of Covestro. The company produces ammonia, urea ammonium nitrate (UAN), and both high-density ammonium nitrate (HDAN) for mining explosives and low-density ammonium nitrate (LDAN) for agricultural fertilizer. LXU is a micro-cap “baby CF Industries”—a concentrated, higher-beta way to play the nitrogen market compared to diversified giants like CF Industries or Nutrien.

The stock has been weighed down by cyclical concerns around nitrogen pricing normalization and broader commodity market volatility. However, the market appears to be overlooking a powerful structural shift: the Strait of Hormuz blockade and Russian ammonium nitrate export suspensions have removed approximately 27% of global ammonia supply and 35% of global urea flows from the market, creating the tightest nitrogen inventory in years and driving North American benchmark prices for ammonia higher. For a company of LXU’s size, this supply dislocation has an outsized impact on margins and cash flow.

A Natural Moat Built on Geography, Chemistry, and Scale

LXU’s competitive moat, while less visible than that of a technology franchise, is deeply rooted in the physical realities of its business. First, ammonia manufacturing is a capital-intensive, hazardous process that achieves meaningful economies of scale only at high production volumes—creating substantial barriers to new entrants. Building a greenfield nitrogen plant requires hundreds of millions of dollars in capital and years of permitting and construction. Second, LXU benefits from a critical feedstock advantage: its U.S.-based plants use domestically sourced natural gas priced well below the international LNG benchmarks that European and Asian competitors must pay. As global LNG markets tighten due to the Hormuz disruption and the force majeure at QatarEnergy’s Ras Laffan facility, LXU’s cost-of-production advantage widens dramatically—the company’s input costs remain stable while global selling prices are being set by the highest-cost marginal producer. Third, the distribution of HDAN (the mining-grade explosive) creates a regional moat: ammonium nitrate is heavy, hazardous to transport, and subject to strict regulatory oversight, which means domestic producers like LXU enjoy near-total pricing power within their geographic footprint. With U.S. copper and gold mining at peak production levels, demand for HDAN remains robust and largely insulated from import competition.

An Oligopolistic Industry With Limited New Supply

The North American nitrogen industry is highly consolidated, with CF Industries (CF), Nutrien (NTR), and LSB Industries accounting for the vast majority of domestic ammonia, UAN, and ammonium nitrate production. This oligopolistic structure is reinforced by the immense capital requirements and long lead times needed to bring new capacity online—no material greenfield nitrogen capacity has been added in the U.S. in decades. Globally, the supply landscape has become even more constrained: Middle Eastern producers, historically the world’s lowest-cost exporters, are now physically blocked from reaching key markets due to the Hormuz chokepoint, while Russian export suspensions have further tightened trade flows.

The result is a supply–demand dynamic that structurally favors incumbent North American producers. Unlike many commodity industries where new entrants can quickly erode pricing power, the nitrogen market’s combination of regulatory hurdles, capital intensity, and geopolitical supply disruptions creates a prolonged window of elevated profitability for domestic players. Additionally, the threat of substitution is low—nitrogen fertilizer is essential to global food production, and while synthetic urea research continues, no commercially viable alternative has emerged. This consolidated market structure gives LXU meaningful pricing leverage that is unlikely to erode in the near to medium term.

Valuation, Catalysts, and the Free Cash Flow Story

At current levels, LXU trades at an undemanding multiple on forward EBITDA, which we view as attractive given the company's meaningful upside leverage to improving nitrogen pricing. Management has outlined a path to higher earnings power and $200 million in EBITDA under more normalized commodity assumptions, and the current pricing environment—supported by supply disruptions tied to geopolitical developments—suggests the potential to exceed those targets. This would imply a roughly mid-single-digit EV/EBITDA multiple. Importantly, nitrogen fertilizer pricing remains the primary driver of both EBITDA and free cash flow for the business, with higher realized prices flowing through with significant operating leverage given a largely fixed-cost structure. As benchmark prices for ammonia and UAN have moved higher, LXU is positioned to capture meaningful incremental profitability, particularly if pricing strength persists through the key spring application season.

Sustainability

Beyond the near-term nitrogen cycle, LXU has a call option in clean energy: its partnership with Lapis Energy to produce blue ammonia—capturing 450,000 metric tons of CO2 and qualifying for annual 45Q tax credits—is on track for a late 2026 startup. The combination of a favorable commodity backdrop, structural supply constraints, a clean energy transition option, and a tax-shielded balance sheet with net operating losses for the next four to five years, makes LXU a compelling small-cap opportunity for investors willing to underwrite the cyclical risk inherent in nitrogen markets.

Disclosures: The information contained herein represents the opinion of Riverwater and should not be construed as personalized or individualized investment advice. Analysis and opinion expressed in this report are subject to change without notice. Reader should not assume that investments in the securities identified were or will be profitable. Timing differences of purchases and sales may have a modest impact on the actual contribution numbers presented. The holdings identified do not represent all the securities purchased, sold, or recommended. The calculation's methodology along with details on all holding's contribution to the overall account's performance during the measurement period are available upon request. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

The contributions presented are shown on a gross basis and do not reflect the deduction of management fees, or other fees. The deduction of management fees reduces investment returns and would also affect the investment characteristics presented. Each contributor's share of performance is shown for informational purposes only and is not predictive of future performance or investment outcomes.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Imperial Brands: Ripe For Uptick
data.description:
Tobacco stock Imperial Brands PLC (IMBBY, IMBBF) saw a pullback last week when the UK banned cigarette sales for under 17s for life. The stock was down by ~3% on the day. But its bounce-back has been sharp. It's now up by 7% since.

Clearly, the ban hasn't hurt IMBBY, but that doesn't mean it's good days for it, either. Since I last wrote about it in October 2025, its price is still down by 9%. In fact, YTD, it's the only tobacco stock among the big five by market capitalization to see a drop (see chart below). Here, I look at why it has weakened and whether there's upside ahead.

Stretched valuations pull back price...

A look at the IMBBY chart shows that after rising by ~10% up to early February, the stock started sliding downwards anyway. The reason isn't hard to find. The company expects adjusted EPS to grow at "high single digit" percentage this year. Assuming this translates into 8% increase, Imperial Brands would report a figure of GBP 3.4 (USD $4.6) in the current financial year (FY26, year ending September 2026).

At the then price of ~USD $46, this resulted in a forward non-GAAP P/E of almost 10x. With the stock's 5-year average forward non-GAAP P/E at 8.7x, over 10% correction was due as a result. By the third week of March, the stock had indeed dropped as much and then resumed its upward journey.

...followed by disappointing NGP developments

However, Imperial Brands' latest trading update, released on April 13, served another blow, with the stock losing another 10% of its value in the three sessions following the release. Two clear reasons stand out that could have invited investor caution.

First, while the company maintains its outlook for the year (see slide below), it has expressed some reservations. This is evident in the remark that "the potential future impact during the second half remains uncertain," based on the current conditions in the Middle East and their potential impact.

Second, the outlook for its next-generation products [NGP] is disappointing. The company anticipates their net revenue growth to be "around mid-to-high single digit percent" in H1 2026, which is lower than the full-year growth expectation of double-digit growth. It also sees some loss in market share in its biggest five markets and expects "moderately higher" adjusted operating losses. And these developments are expected when the company's outlook hasn't changed. If it were to be reduced, based on the present geopolitics and its impact, NGPs could be impacted even more.

What's positive for the Imperial Brands stock?

The stock might have recovered from the smoking ban in the UK, but it's still trading at some of the lowest levels this year, indicating that the trading update in particular has had a notable impact. Still, there are plenty of positives for the stock that indicate there's more upside ahead than not. Here are four of them.

#1. NGP revenue share can continue rising

First, even with the weakness expected in NGP performance in H1 FY26, the company's unchanged outlook for the full year is still encouraging. It expects "double digit" growth for the segment, which is in line with 12% increase seen in FY25, far total net revenue growth of just 1.9% at actual exchange rates.

At 4.4% in FY25, the segment's contribution to total net revenues is small compared to the company's UK based peer British American Tobacco (BTI), whose new categories business brought in 14% of the revenues in 2025.

But it's to Imperial Brands' credit the the figure has inched up over the years, even if slowly. Just in FY24, it was at 4% and five years ago, in 2020, it was a much smaller 2.5%. Also, at this rate, the contribution would rise to a more substantial 8.7% by 2031 if total net revenues and NGP revenues continue to grow at their past five years' CAGR of 0.8% and ~13% respectively.

#2. Britain's contribution to revenues is limited

Even though its a British company, with a significant 37% market share in the UK, the market's contribution to revenue is so small, there's little risk of the smoking ban snowballing into a bigger drag for IMBBY over time.

The UK brings in only some 6% of the company's total revenues. Of this, a non-trivial proportion is attributable to NGPs, since the company has a 10% market share in the UK. Further, of the potential revenue contribution from under 18s would be small for the foreseeable future anyway considering that 80% of the population is adults in the country.

#3. Dividends can add up

The company's dividends are also something to consider. At 5.65%, the stock's forward dividend yield is slightly higher than the average of 5.33% for the big five tobacco stocks. Over a five year period, the yield on cost is much higher at 10.4%.

With a dividend payout ratio at 50% (with adjusted earnings per share as the denominator), they are sustainable too. This is especially as the company has paid dividends consistently for the past 28 years.

#4. Market multiples look competitive again

Assuming that Imperial Brands' adjusted EPS rises at 8% this year, in line with its current outlook, the forward non-GAAP P/E of 8.1x. This is far lower than the 9.4x level the last I checked.

It's also slightly lower than the stock's five-year average of 8.7x, indicating upside of some 5-10%. Further, compared with the average forward P/E for the five tobacco stocks of 13.9x, IMBBY could have an even bigger upside. Though it bears mentioning that the stock's correction from early February indicates that its fair valuation is seen closer to 10x right now.

What next?

The point is, whichever way the valuations are considered, there's still likely to be an uptick in price moving forward. Sure, there are uncertainties ahead in terms of the potential macroeconomic fallout of the war, but then again, defensive stocks can actually be attractive during challenging times.

Also, even with the pullback in NGPs' performance expected in H1 FY26, the full year outlook remains unchanged. Going by the trends over time, they can also continue to grow their contribution to revenues, allowing for a gradual pivot away from traditional tobacco products.

In the meantime, the stock's dividends and market multiples work towards making it attractive both in the short-term and also over a longer time frame. In fact, I believe the stock is ripe for an uptick right away. I'm retaining a Buy rating on IMBBY.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SFL Corporation: Hercules Is Coming Back, But So Is Valuation Risk
data.description:
The last time I covered SFL Corporation (SFL) was back in late September, just after the company had slashed its quarterly dividend from $0.27 to $0.20. SFL has historically been a high-yield play in shipping, though the cyclical nature of the industry means that such cuts are to be expected, even from a company whose cash flows are secured under multi-year charter agreements. In that case, the dividend cut made sense because Hercules, SFL's oil rig, was idle. Debt also hovered at somewhat uncomfortable levels, and even though SFL had already begun divesting some of its older ships, the reduced asset base didn't really help cash flow either.

Since then, the stock is up about 45%, as the reduced dividend has given the impression that it is more defensible, while the yield remains attractive, and Hercules has secured a path back to work. Therefore, I do still like SFL as a diversified, high-yield shipping play. That said, following the rally over the past few months, I don't believe the risk-reward is generous enough to warrant a rating upgrade.

A Fleet in Transition

Back in the day, I perceived SFL’s older tonnage as a risk to its bottom line. Today, the fleet is quite diversified, but while rates are surging across the board, SFL's long-term contracts haven't really captured much of that upside.

In case you aren't following the industry, containership leasing rates (this is where SFL has most of its exposure) remain at multi-year highs, with the Harpex index pushing toward the 2,300 level.

Tanker rates (where SFL is also highly exposed) have surged as well in recent months due to the situation in the Strait of Hormuz and the overall geopolitical instability.

Even dry bulk rates (and indeed SFL has divested much of its assets here, with only two vessels online) are flirting with the previous 2022 and 2024 highs.

The "volatile but structured" environment has largely played out. But with a weighted-average contract duration of 6.5 years, by the time vessel employment opens for renegotiation, rates could have dipped. The only immediate upside for SFL here is the potential to sell some of its vessels at somewhat higher prices.

However, even that is challenging because a) they still come with long-term contracts capping the buyer's upside, and b) timing is a bit of a gamble. For instance, SFL sold the 2005-built Asian Ace and several aging bulkers, totaling over $200 million last year. However, considering how much dry bulk rates have run since then, the company could have secured a much better deal today had it waited.

Now, to give some props to management, last quarter SFL agreed to sell two 2015-built Suezmax tankers (SFL Ottawa and SFL Thelon), for net proceeds of ~$52 million after debt repayment and profit-sharing arrangements. It also paid to release charters on two 2020-built Suezmaxes, SFL Albany and SFL Fraser, leaving them exposed to a strong spot market while longer-term employment is considered later. But as a reminder, this will prove clever if tanker markets stay firm, less so if rates cool before contracts are fixed.

Regardless, SFL seems to care mostly about locking in cash flows, and not so much about renewing its fleet unlike most of its peers, which does offer some underlying predictability. The recent five-year extension with Maersk, for example, for those three 9,500 TEU container ships alone added another $225 million to the backlog, which now stretches out to 2031.

So even with a reduced operating cash flow of ~$267 million following a smaller asset base, the ~$125 million in dividends was well covered. In fact, last year included two larger pre-dividend-cut payments. This year, dividend payments should amount to just over $100 million if SFL doesn't issue any shares, which, again, should be well covered by operating cash flow.

Hercules Returns, but the Story Still Has Gaps

Of course, the most signifcant change since my last update is Hercules, for which the company finally found some work. The rig has been idle since late 2024 and will likely remain that way for almost another year. Still, last month, SFL announced a drilling contract in Canada with a large investment-grade multinational oil and gas company. The minimum term is 400 days, with an estimated contract value of about $170 million.

However, I just hinted that commencement is expected in Q1 2027. That's not really great, because it leaves SFL with a rather extended "dead zone" for the remainder of 2026. Sure, the rig is being mobilized and prepared, but that will incur more costs for the rest of this year.

I like management’s strategy to use Odfjell Drilling to manage the rig. I think this is smart, as it keeps the technical risk at arm's length. But what might be happening is that the market has priced that 2027 upside already, judging by the stock's rally, and that might leave very little room for error if there are any mobilization delays or if the broader offshore market softens before the commencement begins.

Is SFL's Valuation Fair?

That argument brings me to the valuation. If I am being honest, back in September, despite the fresh dividend cut, the yield still hovered close to 10%. In that sense, despite the more brutal post-cut sentiment, the risk-reward was arguably more skewed to the upside if you believed in the turnaround.

Today, that yield has compressed to roughly 7.2%, and SFL might no longer be the "deep value" play it was then. We are now trading at a forward EV/EBITDA multiple that has crept back into the high-9x range. I find this hardly a bargain for a company still wrestling with high interest expenses and a rig that won't contribute until next year.

For context, my favorite containership stocks, Danaos (DAC), Global Ship Lease (GSL), and Euroseas (ESEA), on which I have consistently shared bullish views, trade at EV/EBITDA multiples below 3.5 despite having much cleaner balance sheets. Don't forget that SFL's debt load remains close to $2.6 billion. Even some of my recent bullish tanker picks like Teekay Tankers (TNK) and Hafnia (HAFN), which do have leverage trade at much lower multiples, despite tanker rates remaining incredibly profitable today.

Q2 Results Preview & What It Would Take To Turn Bullish On SFL

SFL will report its Q2 results on May 13th. I am not really interested in where EPS will land. We could see some non-GAAP adjustments, so I am more interested in how well operating cash flow covers the dividend. It will be key to see whether there will be any cash drag tied to Hercules before its contract starts. I also want to hear from management whether the released Suezmax tankers are finding attractive work and whether more older-asset sales are coming, so that we can have a better idea of how SFL's exposure evolves from here.

But again, more importantly, I would listen for practical Hercules details on the earnings call. As I mentioned earlier, potential mobilization costs and the timing of its new employment contract could prove key factors in SFL's future profitability and dividend coverage. We will also be better able to understand whether SFL's premium valuation is or isn't indeed justified.

Now, for me to turn bullish on the stock, SFL either has to get cheaper and trade at multiples closer to its peers or the Hercules and overall outlook have to become clearer. A cheaper valuation would also mean a higher dividend yield. Something closer to 10% would provide a much wider safety net when it comes to SFL's total return outlook. I would also be more confident in the stock if Hercules moves toward its 2027 start without surprise costs and/or if SFL locks in strong tanker charters to prove to us that it is actually able to capture some of the upside enjoyed by its industry peers.

Conclusion

SFL is likely to be able to sustain the current, reduced dividend, which, at the end of the day, still offers an above-average yield. However, I believe that at today's share price, investors are paying for the Hercules success up front, without any meaningful discount, which is what buying into shipping stocks is all about, in my view. If anything goes wrong with Hercules, SFL stock should have a bad time. If all goes well with the rig, I doubt the stock will sustain a multiple expansion or that management will raise the dividend, given the high debt load. This is why I remain neutral on SFL.

data.title: World Kinect Corporation: Volume-Growth A Necessity To Meet EPS Expectations
data.description:
Intro

Our last commentary on World Kinect Corporation (WKC) was in March 2023, when we rated the US-based Floridian energy management outfit a 'Hold'. Gains have been muted to say the least since our commentary over three years ago, with the company returning a mere 3.6% from a capital-gain standpoint and approximately 13% when we include corresponding dividend distributions.

The problem with multi-year consolidation is that it creates more technical resistance to overcome. As we see below on the long-term chart, shares of WKC have been printing lower highs since 2015. Although shares have managed to hold firm above the $18 level during this period, the long-term pattern points to a multi-year descending triangle, if anything else. The fresh monthly MACD crossover points to a higher probability than the contrary that this pattern will ultimately be resolved on the downside.

In previous commentary, we delved into the WKC's ultra-low margins and the need to boost revenue growth in earnest to increase returns over time. A depressed gross margin of 2.66% and an EBIT margin of 0.64% highlight the dire need for WKC to turn over its capital at a much faster clip than it is doing.

In the company's first quarter numbers (Released 4/23/2026), the GAAP earnings result of $0.50 per share was a convincing beat and gave solid evidence that the company's focused portfolio strategy continues to gain traction. Furthermore, the decision to increase full-year adjusted EPS guidance by approximately $0.45 per share resulted in a double-digit jump in the stock, post the release.

Marine Outperforms In Q1

However, when one delves into WKC's first-quarter report, it is evident that trading conditions can change by the dime in this industry, which can meaningfully affect segment profit/volume tallies in Marine, Aviation, or Land. Although the Aviation segment brought in the lion's share of the adjusted gross profit in the quarter ($138.2 million), it was the marine segment that showed the most improvement, due to strong improvisation strategies and execution, driving volumes forward and boasting an impressive 86% gain in adjusted gross margin alone for the quarter, as we learn below. The question, however, is whether these types of performances can be replicated (where uncertainty surmising elevated volatility and pricing is required), which is very difficult unless the right conditions stack up consistently.

However, in March, conditions shifted quickly with volatility increasing sharply and average bunker prices rising approximately 70% month-over-month. By leveraging our supplier relationships and strong balance sheet, the team did what they do best: executed extremely well, supporting our customers while capturing strong risk-adjusted returns in our core resale business and at our physical inventory locations.

Therefore, due to bullish EPS revisions where earnings are now expected to grow by almost 50% in FY26, resulting in a non-GAAP forward earnings multiple of 10.77, management is pitching its stock based on strong earnings growth coupled with a keen valuation, all things remaining equal. Management has also been quick to point to the elevated level of buybacks that WKC continues to adopt, culminating in $75 in share repurchases already this year.

However, from a long-term perspective (taking the above-mentioned technicals into account), we believe forward-looking growth will not be enough to produce a multi-year technical breakout for the following reason.

Tangible Assets & Shareholder Equity On The Wane

Ultimately, when we study companies which have been consolidating for years, we take note of the following cycle and associated trends. Bullish fundamentals align when WKC has the wherewithal to report sufficient revenues, earnings and cash flow, where this very same cash can be used to invest back into its businesses, which restarts the revenue cycle once more. In the above cycle (which we favour), we desire to see a reducing float (which we have at present) and rising equity, which denotes a growing company where investment stakes become more valuable as a result.

However, if we study the chart below, we see that shareholder equity as well as tangible book value have been on the wane for quite some time now. Management will point to how their portfolio has changed in recent times (Compromising divestitures and acquisitions alike, simplifying the business), but the below trends increase downside risk (negating upside potential) and are a confirmation that WKC continues to struggle creating value for its shareholders.

Aviation Segment Uncertainty Due To Middle East Tensions

Although the aviation segment surprised to the upside, driven by favourable market conditions in the first quarter, continued tensions in the Middle East could impact global supply, affecting volume sales as a result. Management has stated that its wider international activity should be able to withstand something like a supply shock, but given as mentioned the tight margins WKC continues to work from, maintaining volume levels elevated is a prerequisite to keep the income statement ticking over.

We remain confident in the aviation outlook but are closely monitoring the global supply landscape. As we progress through the year, we recognise that if the conflict in the Middle East continues for an extended period, it could begin to more broadly impact global supply and customer demand beyond what has so far been generally contained.

Conclusion

Therefore, to sum up, we are maintaining our 'Hold' rating in WKC due to a lack of predictability (regarding its growth profile), which the market craves, meaningful technical resistance, and a weakening foundation due to the declining trend in the company's tangible book value. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Intel's Comeback Is No Longer A Theory
data.description:
A Turnaround That Is Becoming Harder To Dismiss

Intel (INTC) is being revalued because the old story is clearly no longer valid. The market largely still likes to frame the company as a fallen PC chipmaker. That view is quickly becoming outdated. Intel reported a net revenue increase of 7% YoY to $13.6 billion in Q1. The company also reported gross margin and operating margin figures of 39.4 percent and negative 23.1 percent respectively. The GAAP loss reflected heavy restructuring charges. Non-GAAP net income reached $1.5 billion. The most important point is that revenue growth has returned while supply remains tight.

Management was quick to point out the rapid demand growth for silicon. That matters because Intel is far more than just a cyclical PC rebound. Data Center and AI revenue rose 22% in Q1. Intel Foundry revenue rose 16%. All of this is great news in a market that is starved for AI infrastructure. This is a strong indication that investors should give more attention to the CPU side of the AI buildout.

CPUs Are Moving Back To The Center Of AI

GPUs were basically treated as the only assets in AI for a long time. That assumption can be eroded by the rise of agentic tooling. AI inference also needs memory, networking, storage and general compute. CPUs are placed at the most critical point in the system because they can orchestrate all the work. Reuters reported that demand for Intel central processors from AI service providers was so strong in Q1 that the company sold chips it had originally written off.

Intel Xeon 6 was selected as the host CPU for Nvidia DGX Rubin NVL8 systems. Intel also announced a multiyear collaboration with Google for Xeon processors across workload-optimized instances. This could be a major selling point because hyperscalers will have strong first hand knowledge of what bottlenecks appear inside real AI deployments. It is capitalizing on urgent demand the most directly through server CPUs. It may remain the unavoidable toll booth in which AI systems pass when inference workloads scale. In other words, the company does not even have to participate as the most visible accelerator vendor to benefit from AI infrastructure expansion.

18A Makes Intel A Smart Diversified Bet

Intel 18A is the technical heart of the bullish case. The company released initial Intel Core Ultra Series 3 processors on 18A in late 2025. Intel also expects 18A and related nodes to become the key manufacturing process for multiple generations of future client and server CPUs. Smart diversified bet is the right label for Intel because it is exposed to products and manufacturing at once. The company proceeds to use internal products as proof points for a foundry roadmap. A customer can see Panther Lake in market before trusting Intel Foundry with its own designs.

The foundry thesis is also far more durable in the long-term than a simple CPU cycle. Intel says 18A is ready for full product design starts. Its process page highlights PowerVia and RibbonFET as key innovations. The great thing about Intel is that it invests large amounts of capital in assets that are stubbornly entrenched in the physical world. Using its reserves of capital, it can build capacity that software rivals cannot clone overnight. Its plans to spend in excess of most rivals are tied to Fab 52 and broader U.S. manufacturing expansion. Real attempt is the right description for a strategy aimed at reshaping the foundry market at a scale that really matter.

Policy Support And Packaging Expand The Opportunity

The U.S. government investment further changes the risk profile. Under the 2025 agreement, the government committed $8.9 billion for Intel common stock. That included unpaid CHIPS Act grants and Secure Enclave funding. It followed $2.2 billion in CHIPS grants already received. Pouring support for Intel reflects the fact that advanced domestic logic manufacturing is now viewed as an economic and national security priority. This is incredibly important because the truly scarce resources in semiconductors are not press releases. They are land, tools, engineers, process recipes, packaging capacity, and patient capital.

Intel is positioned to benefit from a plethora of other options beyond ordinary CPU sales. Its Penang assembly and test expansion targets customer products amid rising demand for packaging solutions. The company joined the Terafab project with SpaceX, xAI, and Tesla. It also disclosed a Blueprint for provisioning heterogeneous hardware with SambaNova that combines GPUs, RDUs, and Xeon 6 processors. These are a huge indication of a broader platform strategy. Intel is expanding its reach to the next era through compute, packaging, foundry services, and AI system integration. More demand funneled through advanced packaging should help Intel become more relevant to customers that need capacity and architectural flexibility.

Valuation Looks High Until The Lens Expands

Intel no longer appears optically inexpensive. Shares of Intel skyrocketed so far this year after Q1 reporting. Additionally, the company’s stock price is currently trading at approximately 90X its forward estimated earnings. Thus, it can be seen that a portion of the increase in Intel is now reflected in the current stock price.

While expanding your view of the landscape of these technologies, Intel will appear more as a strategic infrastructure platform rather than a "turnaround" lottery ticket. As mentioned previously, Nvidia receives all of the attention (and thus accolades) from investors as it relates to its role in providing the primary AI hardware. However, Nvidia is also the recognized leader of accelerator hardware. While many believe the market has come to recognize the significance of Nvidia's dominance in AI related applications, the market still seems unable to adequately grasp how significantly value is likely to transition quickly to the areas of: limited availability of fabrication capacity; advanced packaging; and infrastructure associated with host CPUs.

Risks And Challenges Are Real

The risk case here isn't insignificant. Intel's potential for very uneven future revenue growth is a major concern due to several factors. Foundry customers don't just magically appear. It can take time to see improvements in manufacturing yields. And factory loading can vary widely. There will also be competition with TSMC as the largest player in the increasingly large and more competitive foundry space. Both AMD and Arm will also continue to put downward pressure on the percentage of x86 architecture use. The bear camp continues to warn of execution risk. The bears aren't always wrong. If Intel fails to meet product delivery deadlines or looses the trust of hyperscalers, then that momentum can slow down too. One of the biggest risks of making would be believing that a single strong quarter eliminates all manufacturing risk.

Also, there are other issues regarding valuation. An attractive entry point for a five year investor in the stock may have some relative volatility before stabilizing after a big run-up. Reuters wrote that brokerages raised their price target after Q1 results were released; however, they also stated that Intel had a significantly higher multiple on its forward earnings estimates compared to AMD and NVDA. This difference in multiples exists due to the fact that investors are willing to pay high premiums for evidence that Intel has transitioned from repair mode to growth mode. If this extremely aggressive expansion plan does indeed slow down, the stock could rapidly re-price.

Why I Am Still Highly Bullish

The bullish view is not simply praying that Intel returns to its old dominance. It is based on a new setup. AI workloads are becoming more and more distributed. Inference is spreading across data centers, enterprise software, edge devices, and PCs. Intel is cementing itself as the rare company with product revenue, foundry optionality, packaging relevance, and government alignment. That combination is more durable in the long-term than a single chip cycle. This takes patience. It also takes a willingness to look past the rather uninspired argument that Intel is only a slow-growth legacy semiconductor company.

In just a few years time, Intel has moved from damaged narrative to credible strategic platform. The company is preparing for a deep structural explosion in compute demand. It has hands-on knowledge and experience across design, manufacturing, packaging, and enterprise customer relationships. This reasoning becomes even more undeniable when investors consider that most AI bottlenecks are physical. Power, capacity, packaging, and supply resilience cannot dramatically decrease over time in importance if AI demand keeps compounding. Most obvious beneficiaries of this trend are companies tied to scarce infrastructure. Intel further establishes itself as one of the most compelling companies in the world if 18A ramps, Xeon demand remains firm, and foundry customers deepen. That is why I view Intel as a highly bullish opportunity despite obvious execution risk.

data.title: Ares Capital: The King Has Spoken And Most Bear Arguments Have Been Answered
data.description:
Ares Capital (ARCC) is one of the first BDCs, which open the earnings season with its own financial performance filings.

Given that ARCC is also the largest BDC in the publicly traded peer complex, its earnings serve as a decent barometer for understanding the overall temperature in the system.

Just for the context. ARCC's market cap is around $13.4 billion, while the second largest peer - Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC) - has "only" $5.6 billion. The BDC sector median market is ~$790 million.

Furthermore, since ARCC has certain characteristics (see below), it makes it even greater source for understanding the direction of travel in the BDC market (especially for those, who are looking for bearish arguments):

  • 24% of the portfolio is placed in SaaS.
  • 14.8% of total income comes from PIK (above sector average of 8.5%).
  • About 40% of the loan book is tied to non-first-lien investments, which, theoretically, should introduce above average risks.
  • The portfolio is spread across lower middle market, core MM an upper MM segments, which means that via ARCC's earnings we're getting a good read of the entire BDC land.

Now, today, going into the Q1 2026 earnings party, ARCC is trading at P/NAV of 0.91x, which is above the sector average of 0.79x. So, despite the optically weaker than average portfolio characteristics and a pre-communicated $0.01 per share drag to the NII generation, ARCC is still priced well-above the market.

In other words, ARCC has a decent chip on its shoulder to not only send the necessary signal to the market that the fears around private credit have been exaggerated, but also prove that it is a premium player.

Without further ado, let's dive into the freshly baked earnings deck.

Earnings review

All in all, ARCC's Q1 2026 earnings are rock-solid across the board and go directly against the bear case that private credit is about to blow up.

The NII per share, which is the core metric to assess BDC cash generation and thus its ability to cover the dividend has improved. It has increased from $0.52 per share in Q4 2025 to $0.55 per share now. This has happened even against the backdrop of interest-rate driven headwinds (as pre-communicated by the management in Q4 earnings call). It was less driven by the top-line expansion, but more by the decreasing expenses associated with lower lower incentive fees (only logical given the stagnant NAV development).

As a result of the improving NII per share, the base dividend coverage has strengthened accordingly. The base dividend coverage has jumped to 114%, which is significantly above the sector average of 98%. More importantly, it allows ARCC to enjoy a decent retained cash generation from which to fund further portfolio growth (or use it as a shock-absorber in case of emerging non-accruals).

Here it is worth noting that there is a notable divergence between GAPP NII per share and GAAP EPS ($0.13 vs. $0.47 per share, respectively). I wouldn't be worried about it because of a) such kind of divergences are inherently characteristic to BDCs and b) the same pattern has been evident for ARCC in the previous quarters. The main difference is that the Core EPS reflects, among other things, net increase (decrease) in equity from net realized and unrealized gains and losses and any capital gains incentive fee attributable to such net realized and unrealized gains and losses, which as I stated above have came in at a higher levels than before. The key is to focus on NAV per share and NII per share dynamics (this is industry's best practice).

The other metric, which is critical for BDCs and investors in the context of measuring portfolio quality and NAV sustainability, is NAV per share. Here, however, we do see indeed a contraction.

This quarter the NAV per share landed at $19.59, which is down from $19.94 in the previous quarter. So, we are talking about ~1.8% correction.

The reason for this is very straightforward: a build-up in the unrealized losses. These losses came in at negative $306 million, which is a meaningful increase relative to the Q4 period (negative $77 million).

Part of it is driven by select markdowns, which would be only reasonable against the backdrop of more challenging private credit environment (e.g., rising energy costs, wider spreads and increased risks in the SaaS sector).

To put it differently, these losses stem from a portfolio-wide markdown activity, which accounts for a structural increase in risks in the private credit space. As I will now discuss about non-accruals, the fact that there haven't been any meaningful loan defaults, it is just another sign that the unrealized losses are related to loan investment value repricingins (not actual defaults). Stable internal risk ratings confirm this as well.

And part of it is driven by an uptick of non-accruals, which this quarter represented 2.1% of the total investments at amortized versus 1.8% in the prior quarter.

However, here is the thing:

  1. Non-accruals on a fair value basis remain perfectly stable (at 1.2%).
  2. The rate of change on a cost basis is immaterial (especially in the context of what the BDC bears have been plotting).
  3. The key driver has been the markdown activity, which doesn't necessarily indicate a distress.

What matters more is the realized gains or losses (the final effect of loan leaving ARCC's system). And here ARCC has delivered a net realized gain of $106 million. In Q4 2025, ARCC had a net realized loss of $170 million.

The PIK has largely remained unchanged, landing at 15.2% of the total income, which is actually below the level that ARCC had in the Q1 - Q3, 2025 period.

Moreover, we have to appreciate the fact that ARCC's debt-to-equity has remained in a solid shape. The debt-to-equity is 1.14x, which is nicely below the sector average of 1.25x.

Now, having said all of this, let me introduce a very important angle, which could probably reduce the overall level of optimism here.

I have isolated ARCC's loans, which were present in 10-K (December 31, 2025) and which had maturity dates in Q1 and Q2 this year. Then I have taken a look at the most recent filings to see what has happened to them.

The picture could be better, to say the least:

Company (Q4, 2025) Sector Pricing Maturity Outcome
Professional Fighters Sports, Media and Entertainment 14% PIK Jan-26 Extended
VPROP Operating Energy 13.46% PIK Jan-26 Extended
NAS and Nationwide Marketing Group Commercial and Professional Services 10.51% Apr-26 Extended
Flinn Scientific and WCI-Quantum Holding Consumer Services 9.22% Apr-26 Extended
Safe Home Security Consumer Services 10.43% (1.00% PIK) May-26 Extended
Absolute Dental Group Health Care Equipment and Services 9.59% PIK Jun-26 Unchanged

The outcome column is what matters here. And this is where we see that all of these loans (except Absolute Dental), which had to mature in Q1 2026 or have maturity dates stretching into Q2 2026 period have been extended.

Not repaid, but extended. Most of them have a PIK component, which makes the things more suspicious (e.g., ARCC kicking the can down the road or "extending and pretending"). And none of these loans are associated with SaaS. The first SaaS loan investment will be maturing September 2026 - "Elemica Parent, Inc. & EZ Elemica Holdings, Inc.".

Of course, it very well might not be the case that these loans have financial difficulties. We have to consider that BDC business is a long-term relationship business, where borrowers tend to refinance their maturing loans with the same BDC. It is similar to the practice we see in the public debt markets, where companies issue new bonds to refinance their existing ones.

This is just a risk that we have to assume.

Now, let me summarize.

The bottom line

There is no question that ARCC has yet again delivered on its promise. This report has clearly shown that the fears of non-accruals spiraling out of control and PIK surging higher lack a fundamental basis.

Granted, there are signs of weakening as implied by the unrealized losses. But these dynamics do not come even close to justifying the current discounts.

When it comes to ARCC, I'm maintaining my previous buy rating with a full expectation that the base dividend will remain well-covered going forward. The improving NII per share generation coupled with a meaningful discount makes it a solid case to include in yield-seeking portfolios.

However, as many of my closest follower have probably noticed it, I am a structural bull on internally managed BDCs, which a) don't have to pay hedge fund type fees (base management and incentive fees); b) tend to trade at premium over NAV, where the shares could be used as a currency to fund growth in an accretive manner; and c) are not dictated by the sponsor alternative asset manager like Ares Management (ARES) for ARCC, which can, theoretically, collide with long-term (and prudent) value creation strategy.

In other words, I would recommend treating ARCC as a tactical position in the overall BDC portfolio.

data.title: Alphabet: Preparing For 'New Age' Q1 Earnings
data.description:
Alphabet Inc. aka Google's (GOOG, GOOGL) earnings are on deck for Wednesday, April 29th.

The stock is up a mesmerizing 115% over the past year, with effectively every single line of business crushing expectations, driving a complete one-eighty in market sentiment, leading to a sharp re-rating in the valuation multiple.

We called the inflection point back in June 2025, and we've continued to pound the table on further significant upside ahead.

Now, let's dive into the key factors to monitor in the upcoming report.

The Google Investment Thesis, In A Nutshell

I recently published a comprehensive write-up detailing my long-term investment thesis in Google, and why I continue to believe it's a Strong Buy and a must-own in concentrated portfolios.

In short, I argue that Google is uniquely positioned to benefit from AI, and that even after the stock surge, the market is far from realizing just how big of a push AI is going to be for Google.

First, the "Why." Alphabet is the only company with leading capabilities across every layer of the AI technology stack, from chips (TPUs) and networking, to research (DeepMind), to a complete cloud suite (GCP), and the most expansive portfolio of services with billions of users (Search, YouTube, Android, Gemini, etc.).

Second, the "How." Google is already leveraging its advantages to drive very real business outcomes, seeing significant acceleration in cloud revenue growth, a huge expansion in Search queries, and meaningful adoption of AI services like Gemini, AI Overviews, and AI Mode.

The way I see it, we're in the very early innings of AI-led acceleration across the entire Google business, and I define the upcoming Q1 earnings report as a potential "Welcome To The New Age" quarter.

All Arrows Point To Broad-Based Acceleration

In Wednesday's earnings, Alphabet is expected to report $107 billion in revenues, reflecting an astounding 18.6% growth, and $2.6 in EPS. As is, Google is expected to deliver the highest growth rate since 2021.

And yet, I argue that it doesn't fully capture the opportunity.

Search & Gemini

In the early days of ChatGPT, the consensus view was that Search is dead. As it turns out, AI and LLMs are the biggest expansion drivers for Google Search since mobile, and the numbers show it.

In Q4, Google Search Paid Clicks growth reached 11%, the highest level since 2022, and bear in mind that it's at a much larger scale today. The one and only explanation for this is that people are turning to Google for more queries, more often, and that they find results more relevant, leading to a click.

In this upcoming report, Google Search is facing very easy comps vs. the 9.8% growth in Q1-25, and I see a 19%+ result as highly likely.

YouTube & Subscriptions

So far, we can note three clear areas where AI has led to meaningful revenues for companies. One is in coding, and the others are advertising and subscriptions. I find coding to be an untapped opportunity for Google and view it as a "free option." In contrast, in ads and subscriptions, Google is already yielding results.

In Q4, YouTube Ads were a disappointing outlier, with growth decelerating sharply. The deceleration was quite surprising relative to competitors like Meta (META) or Google's other advertising businesses.

Management noted headwinds from tough comps from political spending in Q4-24, and strong YouTube Premium adoption, which is shown in the Subscriptions, Platforms & Devices revenue line.

In this upcoming quarter, I expect YouTube to accelerate meaningfully, as comps become easier (elections ended in Q4-24), and engagement was at an all-time high.

Google Cloud

Saving the best for last. Google Cloud is, really, firing on all cylinders.

In the recent Cloud Next event, Google disclosed Gemini Enterprise has grown 40% Q/Q, and that Gemini API is now processing 16bn tokens a minute, up from 10bn last quarter. Further, they now have 330 customers that processed over 1tn tokens in the 12 months that ended in April, and 35 customers that reached the 10tn milestone.

They also launched the new generation TPUs, unveiled an expansive security suite incorporating Wiz, and announced a 5GW commitment from Anthropic (ANTHRO).

In Q4, Google Cloud shocked investors with revenue growth accelerating to 48%, and Google Cloud backlog jumping to $240bn, up 160% Y/Y, with nearly $103 billion in new commitments for the quarter.

In the upcoming quarter, Google Cloud stands to benefit from 2 points of easier comps, continued capacity expansion, and insatiable demand. I expect to see a 5-handle on the revenue growth number and another huge quarter for backlog growth.

Valuation & Why Google's P/E Is Misleading

At $348 per share, Google trades at 30 times forward earnings and 26 times next year's estimates, seemingly the highest levels of the past decade.

However, there's an issue with evaluating hyperscalers based on P/E at the moment, Google included.

On one hand, AI skeptics argue that the historical capex push is being done with no clear ROI, and therefore, by focusing on P/E, investors are missing the fact that on a free cash flow basis, Google and its hyperscaler peers are trading at surreal valuations.

On the other hand, those who think that the ROI question has already been answered (and I include myself among them, see my analysis here, and here), see things much differently.

Because capex is translating into depreciation on the P&L, and because capex is rising far ahead of revenues (Google is roughly doubling its capex in 2026), I find that using a P/E multiple is misleading in the opposite way.

That's why, at this stage of the cycle, I find EBITDA (or operating cash flow) to be the most accurate way to evaluate Google's valuation at the moment. And, based on a forward EV / EBITDA of 19x, we can see that Google is actually trading below its historical average.

In the early innings of a multi-year acceleration period, I find the current setup as highly attractive, with a price target of $420 per share, based on a 19x multiple on 2027 EBITDA.

Conclusion

Alphabet is set to report earnings this Wednesday, April 29th.

I estimate this will be a "Welcome To The New Age" quarter, where the last remaining skeptics will be finally convinced. Moreover, I expect that even Google bulls will be surprised by the sharp acceleration, with the market still underestimating the size of the opportunity ahead.

As such, I reiterate Google at a Strong Buy ahead of earnings.
